prompt.name: optimized-tests
prompt.content:
│ │ # Storybook Setup │ ## Project Info │ | Property | Value | │ |----------|-------| │ | Version | 10.4.0-alpha.10 | │ | Renderer | @storybook/react | │ | Framework | @storybook/react-vite | │ | Builder | @storybook/builder-vite | │ | Config Dir | `.storybook` | │ | CSF Format | CSF Factory | │ | Addons | @storybook/addon-onboarding, @storybook/addon-themes, │ @storybook/addon-docs, @storybook/addon-designs, │ @storybook/addon-vitest, @storybook/addon-a11y, │ storybook-addon-pseudo-states, @chromatic-com/storybook | │ Attention: The following instructions must be followed in order to │ successfully set up Storybook in this project. Do not skip steps or │ attempt to do them out of order. │ Your goal is to make Storybook fully functional in this project by │ analyzing the codebase, │ configuring the preview with the right decorators, and writing stories │ for some components. │ The end state should be a Storybook where any component — from a small │ button to a full page — can be added without story-specific │ workarounds. All necessary providers, CSS, browser state, and network │ mocks should live in the shared preview so that just rendering the │ component in the story is enough. │ After each created story, run Vitest to verify it renders. │ If the test fails, read the error, fix the issue, and re-run until it │ passes before moving on. │ - Copy real patterns from the codebase │ - Keep the app code unchanged │ - Put the default setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx` │ - Keep app mocking and runtime setup in `.storybook/preview.tsx`, not │ in the stories │ ### Storybook Documentation Reference │ Use the following references to look up Storybook APIs, concepts, or │ examples: │ - Full docs index: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/llms.txthttps://storybook.js.org/llms.txt]8;; │ - See code snippets only with codeOnly=true param e.g. │ ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=truehttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=ts&codeOnly=true]8;; │ Key documentation pages for this task: │ - Writing stories: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories...]8;; │ - Decorators: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/decorators.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/deco...]8;; │ - Args: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/args.md?re...]8;; │ - Play functions: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/play-function.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-stories/...]8;; │ - Vitest integration: ]8;;https://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-tests/vitest-plugin.md?renderer=react&language=tshttps://storybook.js.org/docs/10/writing-test...]8;; │ Fetch these URLs directly when you need guidance on Storybook APIs or │ patterns. │ ### Step 1: Analyze the codebase │ Read enough of the app to understand the full runtime environment │ before writing any stories. │ Do not stop at `main.tsx` or `App.tsx`. │ Follow imports into providers, pages, hooks, and shared components │ until you know: │ - which providers exist │ - which CSS files are injected │ - which queries fetch data │ - which browser-state reads happen │ - which portals and portal roots exist │ - which pages and components show the real usage patterns │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // src/main.tsx │ import "./index.css"; │ import App from "./App"; │ import { SessionProvider } from "./contexts/SessionContext"; │ createRoot(document.getElementById("root")!).render( │ │ │ , │ ); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should copy: │ - the `index.css` import │ - the `SessionProvider` │ - the same provider order │ Example of tracing the app deeper: │ ```tsx │ // src/App.tsx │ function App() { │ const { products, loadMoreProducts } = useProducts(); │ const { currentUser, signOut } = useSession(); │ // ... │ } │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useProducts.ts │ const response = await fetch(apiBaseUrl + "/products?page=1"); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // src/hooks/useTheme.ts │ const savedTheme = localStorage.getItem("theme"); │ ``` │ That means the default Storybook setup should discover and prepare: │ - provider state │ - MSW handlers for queries │ - browser-state values that are actually read during render │ ### Step 2: Build one default app environment in preview │ Set up Storybook once so most stories work without story-specific │ setup. │ Start with the smallest faithful environment: │ - the real provider tree │ - the real root CSS │ - seeded browser state if the app reads it during render │ - MSW for network/data queries │ It is fine to seed browser state such as `localStorage`, │ `sessionStorage`, and cookies when the app reads them during render. │ Seed only the specific app-owned keys and values you need. │ Do not clear all `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`, or cookies, and do │ not reset Storybook's own state. │ Do not mock or redefine the browser runtime itself. │ The stories run in Vitest browser mode, so the real browser │ environment should already exist. │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import '../src/index.css'; // import global styles │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { SessionProvider } from '../src/contexts/SessionContext'; │ export default definePreview({ │ decorators: [ │ (Story) => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ ], │ async beforeEach() { │ localStorage.setItem('theme', 'dark'); │ localStorage.setItem('sidebar:open', 'true'); │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use this same idea for: │ - providers │ - root CSS │ - browser state │ - dates, and if the app logic depends on them during render then │ always use `mockdate` │ Example with the `mockdate` package: │ ```tsx │ import MockDate from 'mockdate'; │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ export default definePreview({ │ async beforeEach() { │ MockDate.set('2024-04-01T12:00:00Z'); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 3: Support portals with preview-body.html │ If the app uses portals, copy that setup into Storybook too. │ Look for patterns like: │ - `createPortal(...)` │ - modal, dialog, drawer, popover, tooltip, toast, or dropdown portal │ components │ - hard-coded roots such as `#portal-root`, `#modal-root`, │ `#drawer-root`, or `#toast-root` │ Example of what to copy: │ ```tsx │ // real component │ return createPortal(, │ document.getElementById("portal-root")!); │ ``` │ That means Storybook should create the same portal root in │ `.storybook/preview-body.html`: │ ```html │ │
│ ``` │ If the app uses multiple portal roots, create all of them there: │ ```html │ │
│
│
│ ``` │ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra │ roots for it. │ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, │ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. │ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally │ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook │ environment. │ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. │ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create │ the worker file. │ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so │ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. │ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. │ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. │ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use │ that package manager for the change. │ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: │ ```ts │ // real app hook │ const response = await fetch( │ apiBaseUrl + │ "/products?" + │ new URLSearchParams({ │ page: "1", │ sort: "featured", │ }), │ ); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts │ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; │ export const mswHandlers = { │ products: [ │ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; │ () => │ HttpResponse.json({ │ items: [ │ { │ id: "product-1", │ name: "Example product", │ description: "Mock product description", │ imageUrl: │ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; │ price: 42, │ }, │ ], │ }), │ ), │ ], │ }; │ ``` │ ```tsx │ // .storybook/preview.tsx │ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; │ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; │ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; │ initialize({ │ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', │ }); │ export default definePreview({ │ loaders: [mswLoader], │ parameters: { │ msw: { │ handlers: mswHandlers, │ }, │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ```ts │ // .storybook/main.ts │ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ const config: StorybookConfig = { │ staticDirs: ["../public"], │ }; │ export default config; │ ``` │ Keep these mocks global. │ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. │ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the │ stories actually use. │ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. │ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup │ instead. │ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, │ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. │ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. │ ### Step 5: Write stories │ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. │ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level │ reusable components up to page components. │ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has │ fewer meaningful targets. │ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: │ - pages │ - app shells │ - routes │ - tests │ - existing feature code │ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports │ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. │ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story │ exports in one file. │ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. │ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated │ code. │ #### Story tags │ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify │ AI-created stories: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the │ test still fails │ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside │ `ai-generated`: │ ```ts │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: SomeComponent, │ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], │ }); │ ``` │ #### Args vs render │ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` │ stories — no `render` function needed: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Primary = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ export const Disabled = meta.story({ │ args: { │ variant: 'primary', │ disabled: true, │ children: 'Save', │ }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component │ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { Button } from './Button'; │ import { Card } from './Card'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: Button, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const InsideCard = meta.story({ │ render: () => ( │ │ │ │ ), │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i │ })).toBeVisible(); │ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. │ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. │ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or │ helpers. │ Do not create new application components. │ Do not add a custom `title`. │ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more │ meaningful components or pages available. │ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story │ Every named story export must have a `play` function. │ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. │ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually │ works in the copied Storybook environment: │ - the story renders something real and non-empty │ - the decorators provide the needed context │ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible │ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used │ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave │ correctly │ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. │ A story without assertions is incomplete. │ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: │ - `expect` │ - `waitFor` │ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. │ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = │ within(canvasElement)`. │ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ export const FilledForm: Story = { │ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { │ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { │ selector: "input", │ }); │ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { │ delay: 100, │ }); │ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

content.thinking: Let me start by analyzing the codebase to understand the project structure, providers, CSS, data fetching patterns, and components.

input.prompt:
Explore this project thoroughly. I need to understand:

1. Project structure - what's in src/, what framework, what package manager (check for lock files)
2. Entry point: read src/main.tsx or src/index.tsx
3. App component: read src/App.tsx fully
4. All providers/contexts used (follow imports from main and App)
5. All CSS files imported at the root level
6. Data fetching patterns - how does the app fetch data? (REST, GraphQL, react-query, etc.)
7. Browser state reads (localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies)
8. Portal usage (createPortal, modal roots, etc.)
9. Routing setup
10. List ALL components and pages in the project with their file paths

Be very thorough - read the actual files, don't just list them. I need the content of main.tsx, App.tsx, all context/provider files, and routing setup.

1→import { StrictMode } from 'react'
     2→import { createRoot } from 'react-dom/client'
     3→
     4→import App from './App'

     6→
     7→const container = document.querySelector('#root') as HTMLElement

     9→
    10→root.render(

1→import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
     2→import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
     3→import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
     4→import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'
     5→
     6→import { store } from './app-state'
     7→import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
     8→import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
     9→import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'
    10→
    11→export default function App() {
    12→  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
    13→  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme
    14→
    15→  return (
    16→    <Router>
    17→      <StoreProvider store={store}>
    18→        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
    19→          <GlobalStyle />
    20→          <AppRoutes />
    21→        </ThemeProvider>
    22→      </StoreProvider>
    23→    </Router>
    24→  )
    25→}
    26→

1→import { useLayoutEffect } from 'react'
     2→import { Routes, Route, useLocation } from 'react-router-dom'
     3→
     4→import { CategoryListPage } from './pages/CategoryListPage'
     5→import { CategoryDetailPage } from './pages/CategoryDetailPage'
     6→import { RestaurantDetailPage } from './pages/RestaurantDetailPage'
     7→import { HomePage } from './pages/HomePage'
     8→import { CheckoutPage } from './pages/CheckoutPage'
     9→import { SuccessPage } from './pages/SuccessPage'
    10→
    11→export const AppRoutes: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<unknown>> = ({ children }) => {
    12→  const location = useLocation()
    13→  // Scroll to top when a path changes
    14→  useLayoutEffect(() => {
    15→    window.scrollTo(0, 0)
    16→  }, [location.pathname])
    17→
    18→  return (
    19→    <>
    20→      <div id="modal" />
    21→      <Routes>
    22→        <Route path="/categories" element={<CategoryListPage />} />
    23→        <Route path="/categories/:id" element={<CategoryDetailPage />} />
    24→        <Route path="/restaurants/:id" element={<RestaurantDetailPage />} />
    25→        <Route path="/checkout" element={<CheckoutPage />} />
    26→        <Route path="/success" element={<SuccessPage />} />
    27→        <Route path="/" element={<HomePage />} />
    28→        {children}
    29→      </Routes>
    30→    </>
    31→  )
    32→}
    33→

1→import { createSlice, CaseReducer, PayloadAction } from '@reduxjs/toolkit'
     2→
     3→import { CartItem } from '../cart'
     4→
     5→export type OrderState = {
     6→  items: CartItem[]
     7→}
     8→
     9→const saveOrder: CaseReducer<OrderState, PayloadAction<CartItem[]>> = (state, action) => {
    10→  state.items = action.payload
    11→}
    12→
    13→const clearOrder: CaseReducer<OrderState> = (state) => {
    14→  state.items = []
    15→}
    16→
    17→const initialState: OrderState = {
    18→  items: [],
    19→}
    20→
    21→const orderSlice = createSlice({
    22→  name: 'order',
    23→  initialState,
    24→  reducers: {
    25→    saveOrderAction: saveOrder,
    26→    clearOrderAction: clearOrder,
    27→  },
    28→})
    29→
    30→// Extract the action creators object and the reducer
    31→export const { actions, reducer } = orderSlice
    32→
    33→// Extract and export each action creator by name
    34→export const { saveOrderAction, clearOrderAction } = actions
    35→
    36→// Export the reducer, either as a default or named export
    37→export default reducer
    38→

1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'
     2→
     3→import type { Restaurant } from '../types'
     4→
     5→import { api } from './index'
     6→
     7→export const useFetchRestaurants = () => {
     8→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
     9→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    10→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    11→
    12→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    13→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    14→  }, [setRetry])
    15→
    16→  useEffect(() => {
    17→    setStatus('loading')
    18→
    19→    api
    20→      .getRestaurants()
    21→      .then((res) => {
    22→        setStatus('success')
    23→        setRestaurants(res)
    24→      })
    25→      .catch((error) => {
    26→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    27→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    28→      })
    29→  }, [retry])
    30→
    31→  return {
    32→    status,
    33→    restaurants,
    34→    retryRequest,
    35→  }
    36→}
    37→

    42→
    43→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    44→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    45→  }, [setRetry])
    46→
    47→  useEffect(() => {
    48→    if (categoryId) {
    49→      setStatus('loading')
    50→
    51→      api
    52→        .getRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)
    53→        .then((res) => {

    57→        .catch((error) => {
    58→          const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    59→          setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    60→        })
    61→    }
    62→  }, [categoryId, retry])
    63→
    64→  return {
    65→    status,
    66→    restaurants,
    67→    retryRequest,
    68→  }
    69→}
    70→

    93→  }, [id, retry])

1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'
     2→
     3→export const useKey = (key: string, keyDownCb = () => {}, keyUpCb = () => {}): boolean => {
     4→  const [pressed, setPressed] = useState(false)
     5→  const match = useCallback((event: any) => key.toLowerCase() === event.key.toLowerCase(), [key])
     6→
     7→  const onKeyDown = useCallback(
     8→    (event: KeyboardEvent) => {
     9→      if (match(event)) {
    10→        setPressed(true)
    11→        keyDownCb()
    12→      }
    13→    },
    14→    [keyDownCb, match]
    15→  )
    16→
    17→  const onKeyUp = useCallback(
    18→    (event: KeyboardEvent) => {
    19→      if (match(event)) {
    20→        setPressed(false)
    21→        keyUpCb()
    22→      }
    23→    },
    24→    [keyUpCb, match]
    25→  )
    26→
    27→  useEffect(() => {
    28→    globalThis.addEventListener('keydown', onKeyDown)
    29→    globalThis.addEventListener('keyup', onKeyUp)
    30→
    31→    return () => {
    32→      globalThis.removeEventListener('keydown', onKeyDown)
    33→      globalThis.removeEventListener('keyup', onKeyUp)
    34→    }
    35→  }, [key, onKeyDown, onKeyUp])
    36→
    37→  return pressed
    38→}
    39→

228→│ ``` 229→ 230→│ If a library portals directly to `document.body`, do not add extra 231→│ roots for it. 232→│ Make sure the copied page shell, CSS, and layout still allow overlays, 233→│ fixed positioning, and z-index stacking to render correctly. 234→ 235→│ ### Step 4: Mock side effects globally 236→ 237→│ All network/data queries should be handled by the default Storybook 238→│ environment. 239→ 240→│ - Always use `msw-storybook-addon` for query mocking. 241→│ - If you introduce MSW, run `npx msw init ./public --save` to create 242→│ the worker file. 243→│ - Make sure Storybook serves `./public` as a static dir so 244→│ `mockServiceWorker.js` is available. 245→│ - Do not mock `fetch` directly. 246→│ - Network/data queries should return deterministic mock data. 247→│ - If you need to change dependencies, first check the lockfile and use 248→│ that package manager for the change. 249→ 250→│ Example of copying a real fetch pattern into shared handlers: 251→ 252→│ ```ts 253→│ // real app hook 254→│ const response = await fetch( 255→│ apiBaseUrl + 256→│ "/products?" + 257→│ new URLSearchParams({ 258→│ page: "1", 259→│ sort: "featured", 260→│ }), 261→│ ); 262→│ ``` 263→ 264→│ ```ts 265→│ // .storybook/msw-handlers.ts 266→│ import { http, HttpResponse } from "msw"; 267→ 268→│ export const mswHandlers = { 269→│ products: [ 270→│ http.get("]8;;https://api.example.com/products",https://api.example.com/products",]8;; 271→│ () => 272→│ HttpResponse.json({ 273→│ items: [ 274→│ { 275→│ id: "product-1", 276→│ name: "Example product", 277→│ description: "Mock product description", 278→│ imageUrl: 279→│ "]8;;https://images.example.com/product.jpg",https://images.example.com/product.jpg",]8;; 280→│ price: 42, 281→│ }, 282→│ ], 283→│ }), 284→│ ), 285→│ ], 286→│ }; 287→│ ``` 288→ 289→│ ```tsx 290→│ // .storybook/preview.tsx 291→│ import { definePreview } from 'storybook/preview'; 292→│ import { initialize, mswLoader } from 'msw-storybook-addon'; 293→│ import { mswHandlers } from './msw-handlers'; 294→ 295→│ initialize({ 296→│ onUnhandledRequest: 'bypass', 297→│ }); 298→ 299→│ export default definePreview({ 300→│ loaders: [mswLoader], 301→│ parameters: { 302→│ msw: { 303→│ handlers: mswHandlers, 304→│ }, 305→│ }, 306→│ }); 307→│ ``` 308→ 309→│ ```ts 310→│ // .storybook/main.ts 311→│ import type { StorybookConfig } from "@storybook/react-vite"; 312→ 313→│ const config: StorybookConfig = { 314→│ staticDirs: ["../public"], 315→│ }; 316→ 317→│ export default config; 318→│ ``` 319→ 320→│ Keep these mocks global. 321→│ Do not put fetch mocks in individual stories. 322→│ Only add handlers for requests that the shared preview setup or the 323→│ stories actually use. 324→│ Do not add catch-all handlers that can hide unrelated failures. 325→│ If the defaults are not enough, improve the shared default setup 326→│ instead. 327→│ Seed browser state when needed, but do not mock `window`, `document`, 328→│ `navigator`, observers, or similar runtime APIs. 329→│ The only exception is `mockdate` when date-based rendering exists. 330→ 331→│ ### Step 5: Write stories 332→ 333→│ Try to find around 10 good candidate components for story files. 334→│ Write colocated stories for top-level components, from low-level 335→│ reusable components up to page components. 336→│ Write up to 10 story files, or fewer only if the codebase clearly has 337→│ fewer meaningful targets. 338→ 339→│ The stories should use JSX copied from real usage patterns in: 340→ 341→│ - pages 342→│ - app shells 343→│ - routes 344→│ - tests 345→│ - existing feature code 346→ 347→│ As a rule of thumb, each story file should have around 3 story exports 348→│ when the component or page has enough meaningful states. 349→│ It can have more when the real usage supports it, up to 10 story 350→│ exports in one file. 351→ 352→│ Always show all imports explicitly in story and preview files. 353→│ Do not rely on omitted or implied imports in examples or generated 354→│ code. 355→ 356→│ #### Story tags 357→ 358→│ Every story meta must include the `ai-generated` tag to identify 359→│ AI-created stories: 360→ 361→│ ```tsx 362→│ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; 363→│ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; 364→│ import { SomeComponent } from './SomeComponent'; 365→ 366→│ const meta = preview.meta({ 367→│ component: SomeComponent, 368→│ tags: ['ai-generated'], 369→│ }); 370→ 371→│ export const Default = meta.story({ 372→│ render: () => , 373→│ play: async ({ canvas }) => { 374→│ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeVisible(); 375→│ }, 376→│ }); 377→│ ``` 378→ 379→│ If a story could not be fully fixed after the self-healing loop (the 380→│ test still fails 381→│ or the rendering is incomplete), add the `needs-work` tag alongside 382→│ `ai-generated`: 383→ 384→│ ```ts 385→│ const meta = preview.meta({ 386→│ component: SomeComponent, 387→│ tags: ['ai-generated', 'needs-work'], 388→│ }); 389→│ ``` 390→ 391→│ #### Args vs render 392→ 393→│ For simple components where props drive the state, prefer `args` 394→│ stories — no `render` function needed: 395→ 396→│ ```tsx 397→│ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; 398→│ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; 399→│ import { Button } from './Button'; 400→ 401→│ const meta = preview.meta({ 402→│ component: Button, 403→│ tags: ['ai-generated'], 404→│ }); 405→ 406→│ export const Primary = meta.story({ 407→│ args: { 408→│ variant: 'primary', 409→│ children: 'Save', 410→│ }, 411→│ play: async ({ canvas }) => { 412→│ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i 413→│ })).toBeVisible(); 414→│ }, 415→│ }); 416→ 417→│ export const Disabled = meta.story({ 418→│ args: { 419→│ variant: 'primary', 420→│ disabled: true, 421→│ children: 'Save', 422→│ }, 423→│ play: async ({ canvas }) => { 424→│ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled(); 425→│ }, 426→│ }); 427→│ ``` 428→ 429→│ Use `render` when the story needs composition — wrapping the component 430→│ in layout, combining multiple components, or passing children as JSX: 431→ 432→│ ```tsx 433→│ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; 434→│ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; 435→│ import { Button } from './Button'; 436→│ import { Card } from './Card'; 437→ 438→│ const meta = preview.meta({ 439→│ component: Button, 440→│ tags: ['ai-generated'], 441→│ }); 442→ 443→│ export const InsideCard = meta.story({ 444→│ render: () => ( 445→│ 446→│ 447→│ 448→│ ), 449→│ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { 450→│ await expect(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i 451→│ })).toBeVisible(); 452→│ await userEvent.click(canvas.getByRole('button', { name: /save/i })); 453→│ }, 454→│ }); 455→│ ``` 456→ 457→│ Keep app mocking and runtime setup in preview, not in the stories. 458→│ Do not build large story-specific harnesses. 459→│ Do not write story files for subcomponents, hooks, contexts, or 460→│ helpers. 461→│ Do not create new application components. 462→│ Do not add a custom `title`. 463→│ Do not stop after only a few easy targets if the codebase has more 464→│ meaningful components or pages available. 465→ 466→│ ### Step 6: Write a play function for every story 467→ 468→│ Every named story export must have a `play` function. 469→│ The `play` function is not optional, even for simple stories. 470→ 471→│ The purpose of the `play` function is to prove that the story actually 472→│ works in the copied Storybook environment: 473→ 474→│ - the story renders something real and non-empty 475→│ - the decorators provide the needed context 476→│ - the CSS is applied well enough for the intended state to be visible 477→│ - the MSW mocks or seeded browser state are actually being used 478→│ - important interactions, async loading states, and portals behave 479→│ correctly 480→ 481→│ Use `play` functions to verify behavior, not just to click around. 482→│ A story without assertions is incomplete. 483→ 484→│ Use tools from `storybook/test` such as: 485→ 486→│ - `expect` 487→│ - `waitFor` 488→ 489→│ Prefer `canvas` and `userEvent` from the `play` context. 490→│ Do not destructure `canvasElement` just to create `const canvas = 491→│ within(canvasElement)`. 492→│ Do not import `userEvent` from `storybook/test`; use `userEvent` from 493→│ the `play` context instead. 494→│ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside 495→│ the canvas, such as for portals. 496→ 497→│ Example: 498→ 499→│ ```tsx 500→│ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; 501→ 502→│ export const FilledForm: Story = { 503→│ play: async ({ canvas, userEvent }) => { 504→│ const emailInput = canvas.getByLabelText("email", { 505→│ selector: "input", 506→│ }); 507→ 508→│ await userEvent.type(emailInput, "example-email@email.com", { 509→│ delay: 100, 510→│ }); 511→ 512→│ const passwordInput = canvas.getByLabelText("password", { 513→│ selector: "input", 514→│ }); 515→ 516→│ await userEvent.type(passwordInput, "ExamplePassword", { 517→│ delay: 100, 518→│ }); 519→ 520→│ const submitButton = canvas.getByRole("button"); 521→│ await userEvent.click(submitButton); 522→│ }, 523→│ }; 524→│ ``` 525→ 526→│ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: 527→ 528→│ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI 529→│ appears correctly 530→│ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and 531→│ assert on it 532→│ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, 533→│ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles 534→│ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or 535→│ navigation outcome 536→│ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when 537→│ the UI renders outside the canvas 538→ 539→│ Examples of useful checks: 540→ 541→│ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or 542→│ disabled 543→│ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the 544→│ portal root 545→│ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner 546→│ forever 547→│ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel 548→│ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual 549→│ state 550→│ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters 551→ 552→│ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` 553→ 554→│ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific 555→│ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a 556→│ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. 557→ 558→│ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its 559→│ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: 560→ 561→│ ```tsx 562→│ export const CssCheck: Story = { 563→│ args: { children: "Submit" }, 564→│ play: async ({ canvas }) => { 565→│ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); 566→│ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did 567→│ not load. 568→│ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, 569→│ 99, 235)"); 570→│ }, 571→│ }; 572→│ ``` 573→ 574→│ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found 575→ 576→│ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: 577→ 578→│ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage 579→│ - a provider-backed component 580→│ - a browser-state-backed component 581→│ - a fetched-data component 582→│ - a real page component 583→ 584→│ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, 585→│ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page 586→│ components. 587→ 588→│ Example page story: 589→ 590→│ ```tsx 591→│ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; 592→│ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; 593→│ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; 594→ 595→│ const meta = preview.meta({ 596→│ component: ProductPage, 597→│ tags: ['ai-generated'], 598→│ }); 599→ 600→│ export const Default = meta.story({ 601→│ render: () => , 602→│ play: async ({ canvas }) => { 603→│ await expect( 604→│ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), 605→│ ).toBeVisible(); 606→│ }, 607→│ }); 608→│ ``` 609→ 610→│ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types 611→ 612→│ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: 613→ 614→│ ```bash 615→│ npx vitest --project storybook 616→│ ``` 617→ 618→│ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, 619→│ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project 620→│ itself uses. 621→ 622→│ ```bash 623→│ 624→│ ``` 625→ 626→│ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove 627→│ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug 628→│ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval 629→│ artifacts. 630→ 631→│ Keep iterating until: 632→ 633→│ - every story you wrote passes 634→│ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function 635→│ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real 636→│ TypeScript check 637→│ - the rendered output looks sensible 638→│ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories 639→│ do not need manual fetch overrides 640→│ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story 641→│ JSX are fixed 642→│ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta 643→│ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', 644→│ 'needs-work']` in their meta

1→import * as React from 'react'
     2→import { CSSTransition } from 'react-transition-group'
     3→
     4→import { Portal } from '../Portal'
     5→import { useKey } from '../../hooks/useKeyboard'
     6→import { Button } from '../Button'
     7→import { useLockBodyScroll } from '../../hooks/useBodyScrollLock'
     8→
     9→import { ModalContent, TopBar, Backdrop } from './Modal.styles'
    10→
    11→type ModalProps = {
    12→  isOpen: boolean
    13→  onClose: () => void
    14→}
    15→
    16→export const Modal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<ModalProps>> = ({
    17→  children,
    18→  isOpen,
    19→  onClose,
    20→}) => {
    21→  useKey('escape', onClose)
    22→  useLockBodyScroll(isOpen)
    23→  const contentRef = React.useRef(null)
    24→  const backdropRef = React.useRef(null)
    25→
    26→  return (
    27→    <Portal selector="#modal">
    28→      <CSSTransition
    29→        in={isOpen}
    30→        timeout={300}
    31→        classNames="modal"
    32→        unmountOnExit
    33→        nodeRef={contentRef}
    34→      >
    35→        <ModalContent data-testid="modal" ref={contentRef}>
    36→          <TopBar>
    37→            <Button
    38→              data-testid="modal-close-btn"
    39→              onClick={onClose}
    40→              clear
    41→              round
    42→              icon="cross"
    43→              aria-label="close modal"
    44→              iconSize={16}
    45→            />
    46→          </TopBar>
    47→          {children}
    48→        </ModalContent>
    49→      </CSSTransition>
    50→
    51→      <CSSTransition
    52→        in={isOpen}
    53→        timeout={300}
    54→        classNames="backdrop"
    55→        unmountOnExit
    56→        nodeRef={backdropRef}
    57→      >
    58→        <Backdrop data-testid="modal-backdrop" ref={backdropRef} onClick={onClose} />
    59→      </CSSTransition>
    60→    </Portal>
    61→  )
    62→}

Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the project. Let me create a detailed report:

## Project Analysis Report

### 1. Project Structure

**Framework**: React 19 with TypeScript
**Package Manager**: Yarn 4.2.1 (yarn.lock present)
**Build Tool**: Vite 6.2.0
**Testing**: Vitest, Testing Library, Storybook
**State Management**: Redux Toolkit
**Routing**: React Router DOM v7
**Styling**: Styled-components v6
**Forms**: React Hook Form with Zod validation
**Dark Mode**: use-dark-mode library

### 2. Entry Point

**File**: `/src/index.tsx`

The entry point renders the App component in the root element with StrictMode. Service worker registration is disabled. No CSS imports in the entry point.

### 3. App Component

**File**: `/src/App.tsx`

The App component serves as the main provider wrapper with:
- **ThemeProvider** (styled-components) with light/dark theme from `use-dark-mode` library
- **BrowserRouter** (Router from react-router-dom)
- **StoreProvider** (Redux store)
- **GlobalStyle** component
- **AppRoutes** component for routing setup

### 4. Root-Level Providers and Contexts

The app uses the following providers:

1. **Redux Store** (`/src/app-state/index.ts`, `/src/app-state/store.ts`):
   - Two slices: `cart` and `order`
   - Custom hooks: `useAppDispatch()` and `useAppSelector()` for type-safe Redux access
   - State exports from `/src/app-state/hooks.ts`

2. **styled-components ThemeProvider**:
   - Theme switching via `use-dark-mode` (window global mode)
   - Themes in `/src/styles/theme.ts`

3. **React Router**:
   - BrowserRouter for client-side routing
   - Routes in `/src/Routes.tsx`

4. **Global Style** (`/src/styles/GlobalStyle.tsx`):
   - Imports: `react-multi-carousel/lib/styles.css` and `react-loading-skeleton/dist/skeleton.css`
   - Montserrat (400, 500, 700, 900) and Hind (400, 500) Google fonts
   - CSS reset, global transitions, breakpoint utilities, and carousel overrides

### 5. CSS Files at Root Level

**Global imports in GlobalStyle component**:
- `react-multi-carousel/lib/styles.css`
- `react-loading-skeleton/dist/skeleton.css`

**Theme files**:
- `/src/styles/theme.ts` - Theme definitions (light and dark)
- `/src/styles/GlobalStyle.tsx` - Global CSS and font declarations
- `/src/styles/CSSReset.ts` - CSS reset utilities
- `/src/styles/breakpoints.ts` - Responsive breakpoints

All styling uses styled-components with CSS-in-JS pattern.

### 6. Data Fetching Patterns

**Pattern**: REST API with Axios + custom hooks (no react-query or SWR)

**API Layer** (`/src/api/index.ts`):
- Axios-based `RestaurantsApi` class
- Base URL: `https://mealdrop.netlify.app/.netlify/functions/restaurants`
- Built-in client-side caching (Map-based, disabled in test/Storybook environments)
- Methods: `getRestaurants()`, `getRestaurantById(id)`, `getRestaurantsByCategory(category)`

**API Hooks** (`/src/api/hooks.ts`):
- `useFetchRestaurants()` - fetch all restaurants
- `useFetchRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)` - fetch by category
- `useFetchRestaurant(id)` - fetch single restaurant
- Each hook returns: `{ status, restaurants/restaurant, retryRequest }`
- Status values: 'idle', 'loading', 'success', or error status code (as string)
- Retry mechanism via state toggle

### 7. Browser State Reads

**No localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookie usage detected**.

The app only uses:
- `globalThis.window` for `use-dark-mode` global mode setting
- `globalThis.navigator.userAgent` in `isMobile()` helper

### 8. Portal Usage

**Portal Component** (`/src/components/Portal.tsx`):
- Uses `createPortal()` from react-dom
- Accepts a CSS selector string to find target DOM node
- Modal root element: `<div id="modal" />` created in `/src/Routes.tsx`

## Project Analysis Report

### 1. Project Structure

**Framework**: React 19 with TypeScript
**Package Manager**: Yarn 4.2.1 (yarn.lock present)
**Build Tool**: Vite 6.2.0
**Testing**: Vitest, Testing Library, Storybook
**State Management**: Redux Toolkit
**Routing**: React Router DOM v7
**Styling**: Styled-components v6
**Forms**: React Hook Form with Zod validation
**Dark Mode**: use-dark-mode library

### 2. Entry Point

**File**: `/src/index.tsx`

The entry point renders the App component in the root element with StrictMode. Service worker registration is disabled. No CSS imports in the entry point.

### 3. App Component

**File**: `/src/App.tsx`

The App component serves as the main provider wrapper with:
- **ThemeProvider** (styled-components) with light/dark theme from `use-dark-mode` library
- **BrowserRouter** (Router from react-router-dom)
- **StoreProvider** (Redux store)
- **GlobalStyle** component
- **AppRoutes** component for routing setup

### 4. Root-Level Providers and Contexts

The app uses the following providers:

1. **Redux Store** (`/src/app-state/index.ts`, `/src/app-state/store.ts`):
   - Two slices: `cart` and `order`
   - Custom hooks: `useAppDispatch()` and `useAppSelector()` for type-safe Redux access
   - State exports from `/src/app-state/hooks.ts`

2. **styled-components ThemeProvider**:
   - Theme switching via `use-dark-mode` (window global mode)
   - Themes in `/src/styles/theme.ts`

3. **React Router**:
   - BrowserRouter for client-side routing
   - Routes in `/src/Routes.tsx`

4. **Global Style** (`/src/styles/GlobalStyle.tsx`):
   - Imports: `react-multi-carousel/lib/styles.css` and `react-loading-skeleton/dist/skeleton.css`
   - Montserrat (400, 500, 700, 900) and Hind (400, 500) Google fonts
   - CSS reset, global transitions, breakpoint utilities, and carousel overrides

### 5. CSS Files at Root Level

**Global imports in GlobalStyle component**:
- `react-multi-carousel/lib/styles.css`
- `react-loading-skeleton/dist/skeleton.css`

**Theme files**:
- `/src/styles/theme.ts` - Theme definitions (light and dark)
- `/src/styles/GlobalStyle.tsx` - Global CSS and font declarations
- `/src/styles/CSSReset.ts` - CSS reset utilities
- `/src/styles/breakpoints.ts` - Responsive breakpoints

All styling uses styled-components with CSS-in-JS pattern.

### 6. Data Fetching Patterns

**Pattern**: REST API with Axios + custom hooks (no react-query or SWR)

**API Layer** (`/src/api/index.ts`):
- Axios-based `RestaurantsApi` class
- Base URL: `https://mealdrop.netlify.app/.netlify/functions/restaurants`
- Built-in client-side caching (Map-based, disabled in test/Storybook environments)
- Methods: `getRestaurants()`, `getRestaurantById(id)`, `getRestaurantsByCategory(category)`

**API Hooks** (`/src/api/hooks.ts`):
- `useFetchRestaurants()` - fetch all restaurants
- `useFetchRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)` - fetch by category
- `useFetchRestaurant(id)` - fetch single restaurant
- Each hook returns: `{ status, restaurants/restaurant, retryRequest }`
- Status values: 'idle', 'loading', 'success', or error status code (as string)
- Retry mechanism via state toggle

### 7. Browser State Reads

**No localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookie usage detected**.

The app only uses:
- `globalThis.window` for `use-dark-mode` global mode setting
- `globalThis.navigator.userAgent` in `isMobile()` helper

### 8. Portal Usage

**Portal Component** (`/src/components/Portal.tsx`):

- Accepts a CSS selector string to find target DOM node
- Modal root element: `<div id="modal" />` created in `/src/Routes.tsx`

- CSSTransition animations on both content and backdrop
- Used for modals and dialogs

file.content:
import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'

import { store } from './app-state'
import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'

export default function App() {
  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme

  return (
    <Router>
      <StoreProvider store={store}>
        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
          <GlobalStyle />
          <AppRoutes />
        </ThemeProvider>
      </StoreProvider>
    </Router>
  )
}

file.content:
import { DefaultTheme } from 'styled-components'

declare module 'styled-components' {
  export interface DefaultTheme {
    name: 'light' | 'dark'
    fonts: {
      family: string
    }
    spacing: typeof spacing
    color: typeof color
    borderRadius: typeof borderRadius
    boxShadow: typeof boxShadow
    typography: typeof typography
  }
}

const baseColors = {
  white: '#FFFFFF ',
  black: '#202020',
  red: {
    base: '#D70808',
    light1: '#E57373',
    light2: '#EF9A9A',
    dark1: '#C62828',
    dark2: '#B71C1C',
  },
  otherBlack: '#2C2C2C ',
  grey: {
    base: '#909090',
    light1: '#A6A6A6',
    light2: '#BCBCBC',
    light3: '#D2D2D2',
    light4: '#E9E9E9',
    light5: '#F5F6F7',
    light6: '#F9F9F9',
    dark1: '#797979',
    dark2: '#636363',
    dark3: '#4D4D4D',
    dark4: '#363636',
    dark5: '#2C2C2C',
    dark6: '#202020',
  },
  green: {
    base: '#E5F8BC',
    light1: '#E8F9C3',
    light2: '#EAF9C9',
    light3: '#EDFAD0',
    light4: '#EFFBD7',
    light5: '#F2FCDE',
    dark1: '#CEDFA9',
    dark2: '#B7C696',
    dark3: '#A0AE84',
    dark4: '#899571',
    dark5: '#737C5E',
  },
  blue: {
    base: '#B1DDE4',
    light1: '#B9E0E7',
    light2: '#C1E4E9',
    light3: '#C8E7EC',
    light4: '#D0EBEF',
    light5: '#D8EEF2',
    dark1: '#9FC7CD',
    dark2: '#8EB1B6',
    dark3: '#7C9BA0',
    dark4: '#6A8589',
    dark5: '#596f72',
  },
}

const spaceUnit = 1

const borderRadius = {
  xs: '4px',
  s: '8px',
  m: '16px',
  l: '24px',
  xl: '32px',
  xxl: '40px',
  round: '50%',
}

const spacing = {
  xxs: `${0.25 * spaceUnit}em`,
  xs: `${0.5 * spaceUnit}em`,
  s: `${spaceUnit}em`,
  m: `${1.25 * spaceUnit}em`,
  l: `${2 * spaceUnit}em`,
  xl: `${3.25 * spaceUnit}em`,
  xxl: `${5.25 * spaceUnit}em`,
}

const boxShadow = {
  card: '0px 14px 26px 0px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.08)',
  inner: 'inset 0 3px 0 0 rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.05)',
  outerBorder: `0 0 0 1px ${baseColors.blue.dark2}, 0 0 0 4px ${baseColors.blue.light5}`,
}

const typography = {
  fontSize: {
    body: '1.125rem',
    bodyS: '1rem',
    bodyXS: '0.9rem',
    bodyXXS: '0.72rem',
    heading1: '2.74rem',
    heading2: '2.19rem',
    heading3: '1.75rem',
    heading4: '1.4rem',
  },
  fontWeight: {
    black: '900',
    bold: '700',
    medium: '500',
    regular: '400',
  },
}

const color = {
  accentText: baseColors.black,
  badgeBackground: baseColors.grey.light4,
  badgeText: baseColors.grey.dark2,
  cartButtonText: baseColors.grey.base,
  bannerBackground: baseColors.blue.base,
  buttonClear: 'transparent',
  buttonClearHover: baseColors.grey.light5,
  buttonPrimary: baseColors.grey.dark6,
  buttonPrimaryHover: baseColors.grey.dark5, // recheck
  buttonSecondary: baseColors.green.base,
  buttonSecondaryHover: baseColors.green.dark1, // recheck
  buttonText: baseColors.white,
  cardBackground: baseColors.grey.light6,
  checkoutTopBackground: baseColors.green.base,
  checkoutBottomBackground: baseColors.grey.light6,
  error: baseColors.red.dark2,
  formBackground: baseColors.white,
  foodItemBackground: baseColors.white,
  footerBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
  headerBackground: baseColors.white,
  restaurantDetailBackground: baseColors.white,
  headerBorder: baseColors.grey.light4,
  inputBackground: baseColors.grey.light5,
  inputHint: baseColors.grey.dark1,
  inputIcon: baseColors.grey.dark6,
  label: baseColors.grey.dark4,
  labelActive: baseColors.grey.dark6,
  menuSectionBackground: baseColors.grey.light6,
  stepsIndicatorInnerBar: baseColors.grey.dark6,
  stepsIndicatorOuterBar: baseColors.grey.light5,
  orderSummaryBackground: baseColors.white,
  overlayBackground: baseColors.white,
  overlayHeader: baseColors.grey.light5,
  primaryText: baseColors.otherBlack,
  secondaryText: baseColors.grey.dark2,
  // This is just used to demo a11y violations, use it in the Review component
  bannerText: baseColors.blue.dark3,
  reviewText: baseColors.blue.dark5,
  screenBackground: baseColors.white,
  sidebarHeader: baseColors.grey.dark4,
  sidebarFooter: baseColors.white,
  skeletonBase: baseColors.grey.light4,
  skeletonHighlight: baseColors.grey.light5,
  topBannerBackground: baseColors.green.base,
  newRestaurantTag: baseColors.green.base,
  white: baseColors.white,
  black: baseColors.black,
}

export const lightTheme: DefaultTheme = {
  borderRadius,
  boxShadow,
  color,
  fonts: {
    family: 'NunitoSans, sans-serif',
  },
  name: 'light',
  spacing,
  typography,
}

export const darkTheme: DefaultTheme = {
  ...lightTheme,
  boxShadow: {
    ...boxShadow,
    outerBorder: `0 0 0 2px ${baseColors.blue.dark5}, 0 0 0 4px ${baseColors.blue.base}`,
  },
  color: {
    ...lightTheme.color,
    badgeBackground: baseColors.grey.dark4,
    badgeText: baseColors.grey.light1,
    cartButtonText: baseColors.grey.dark2,
    bannerBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    buttonClearHover: baseColors.grey.dark4, // recheck
    buttonPrimary: baseColors.green.base,
    buttonPrimaryHover: baseColors.green.dark1, // recheck
    buttonSecondary: baseColors.green.base,
    buttonText: baseColors.otherBlack,
    cardBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    checkoutTopBackground: baseColors.grey.dark6,
    checkoutBottomBackground: baseColors.grey.dark1,
    formBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    foodItemBackground: baseColors.otherBlack,
    headerBackground: baseColors.grey.dark6,
    restaurantDetailBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    headerBorder: baseColors.grey.dark4,
    inputBackground: baseColors.grey.dark4,
    inputIcon: baseColors.white,
    inputHint: baseColors.grey.light1,
    label: baseColors.grey.light4,
    labelActive: baseColors.white,
    menuSectionBackground: baseColors.grey.dark6,
    stepsIndicatorInnerBar: baseColors.green.base,
    stepsIndicatorOuterBar: baseColors.grey.dark4,
    orderSummaryBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    overlayBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    overlayHeader: baseColors.grey.dark4,
    primaryText: baseColors.white,
    reviewText: baseColors.blue.base,
    screenBackground: baseColors.grey.dark6,
    sidebarHeader: baseColors.grey.dark4,
    sidebarFooter: baseColors.grey.dark5,
    skeletonBase: baseColors.grey.dark3,
    skeletonHighlight: baseColors.grey.dark2,
    topBannerBackground: baseColors.grey.dark5,
  },
  name: 'dark',
}

export type ShopItem = {
  id: number
  name: string
  price: number
  imageUrl?: string
  description?: string
}

export type FoodMenuItem = {
  id: number
  name: string
  description?: string
  price: number
}

export type Restaurant = {
  name: string
  id?: string
  mapsUrl?: string
  rating?: number
  url?: string
  address?: string
  specialty: string
  photoUrl: string
  isClosed?: boolean
  categories?: string[]
  isLoading?: boolean
  isNew?: boolean
  menu: {
    food: FoodMenuItem[]
    dessert: FoodMenuItem[]
    drinks: FoodMenuItem[]
  }
}

1→import { useCallback, useEffect, useState } from 'react'
     2→
     3→import type { Restaurant } from '../types'
     4→
     5→import { api } from './index'
     6→
     7→export const useFetchRestaurants = () => {
     8→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
     9→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    10→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    11→
    12→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    13→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    14→  }, [setRetry])
    15→
    16→  useEffect(() => {
    17→    setStatus('loading')
    18→
    19→    api
    20→      .getRestaurants()
    21→      .then((res) => {
    22→        setStatus('success')
    23→        setRestaurants(res)
    24→      })
    25→      .catch((error) => {
    26→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    27→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    28→      })
    29→  }, [retry])
    30→
    31→  return {
    32→    status,
    33→    restaurants,
    34→    retryRequest,
    35→  }
    36→}
    37→
    38→export const useFetchRestaurantsByCategory = (categoryId?: string) => {
    39→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    40→  const [restaurants, setRestaurants] = useState<Restaurant[]>([])
    41→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    42→
    43→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    44→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    45→  }, [setRetry])
    46→
    47→  useEffect(() => {
    48→    if (categoryId) {
    49→      setStatus('loading')
    50→
    51→      api
    52→        .getRestaurantsByCategory(categoryId)
    53→        .then((res) => {
    54→          setStatus('success')
    55→          setRestaurants(res)
    56→        })
    57→        .catch((error) => {
    58→          const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    59→          setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    60→        })
    61→    }
    62→  }, [categoryId, retry])
    63→
    64→  return {
    65→    status,
    66→    restaurants,
    67→    retryRequest,
    68→  }
    69→}
    70→
    71→export const useFetchRestaurant = (id: string) => {
    72→  const [status, setStatus] = useState('idle')
    73→  const [restaurant, setRestaurant] = useState<Restaurant>()
    74→  const [retry, setRetry] = useState(false)
    75→
    76→  const retryRequest = useCallback(() => {
    77→    setRetry((prev) => !prev)
    78→  }, [setRetry])
    79→
    80→  useEffect(() => {
    81→    setStatus('loading')
    82→
    83→    api
    84→      .getRestaurantById(id)
    85→      .then((res) => {
    86→        setStatus('success')
    87→        setRestaurant(res)
    88→      })
    89→      .catch((error) => {
    90→        const statusCode = error?.response?.status || 500
    91→        setStatus(statusCode.toString())
    92→      })
    93→  }, [id, retry])
    94→
    95→  return {
    96→    status,
    97→    restaurant,
    98→    retryRequest,
    99→  }
   100→}
   101→

1→import * as React from 'react'
     2→import { CSSTransition } from 'react-transition-group'
     3→
     4→import { Portal } from '../Portal'
     5→import { useKey } from '../../hooks/useKeyboard'
     6→import { Button } from '../Button'
     7→import { useLockBodyScroll } from '../../hooks/useBodyScrollLock'
     8→
     9→import { ModalContent, TopBar, Backdrop } from './Modal.styles'
    10→
    11→type ModalProps = {
    12→  isOpen: boolean
    13→  onClose: () => void
    14→}
    15→
    16→export const Modal: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<ModalProps>> = ({
    17→  children,
    18→  isOpen,
    19→  onClose,
    20→}) => {
    21→  useKey('escape', onClose)
    22→  useLockBodyScroll(isOpen)
    23→  const contentRef = React.useRef(null)
    24→  const backdropRef = React.useRef(null)
    25→
    26→  return (
    27→    <Portal selector="#modal">
    28→      <CSSTransition
    29→        in={isOpen}
    30→        timeout={300}
    31→        classNames="modal"
    32→        unmountOnExit
    33→        nodeRef={contentRef}
    34→      >
    35→        <ModalContent data-testid="modal" ref={contentRef}>
    36→          <TopBar>
    37→            <Button
    38→              data-testid="modal-close-btn"
    39→              onClick={onClose}
    40→              clear
    41→              round
    42→              icon="cross"
    43→              aria-label="close modal"
    44→              iconSize={16}
    45→            />
    46→          </TopBar>
    47→          {children}
    48→        </ModalContent>
    49→      </CSSTransition>
    50→
    51→      <CSSTransition
    52→        in={isOpen}
    53→        timeout={300}
    54→        classNames="backdrop"
    55→        unmountOnExit
    56→        nodeRef={backdropRef}
    57→      >
    58→        <Backdrop data-testid="modal-backdrop" ref={backdropRef} onClick={onClose} />
    59→      </CSSTransition>
    60→    </Portal>
    61→  )
    62→}
    63→

1→import * as React from 'react'
     2→import { CSSTransition } from 'react-transition-group'
     3→
     4→import { useKey, useLockBodyScroll } from '../../hooks'
     5→import { Button } from '../Button'
     6→import { Heading } from '../typography'
     7→
     8→import { SidebarContainer, SidebarContent, SidebarFooter, TopBar, Backdrop } from './Sidebar.styles'
     9→
    10→type SidebarProps = {
    11→  isOpen: boolean
    12→  title: string
    13→  onClose: () => void
    14→  footer?: React.ReactNode
    15→}
    16→
    17→export const Sidebar: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<SidebarProps>> = ({
    18→  children,
    19→  footer,
    20→  isOpen,
    21→  title,
    22→  onClose,
    23→}) => {
    24→  useKey('escape', onClose)
    25→  useLockBodyScroll(isOpen)
    26→  const contentRef = React.useRef(null)
    27→  const backdropRef = React.useRef(null)
    28→
    29→  return (
    30→    <>
    31→      <CSSTransition
    32→        in={isOpen}
    33→        timeout={300}
    34→        classNames="sidebar"
    35→        unmountOnExit
    36→        nodeRef={contentRef}
    37→      >
    38→        <SidebarContainer data-testid="sidebar" ref={contentRef}>
    39→          <TopBar>
    40→            <Heading level={4}>{title}</Heading>
    41→            <Button
    42→              aria-label="close sidebar"
    43→              data-testid="sidebar-close-btn"
    44→              onClick={onClose}
    45→              clear
    46→              round
    47→              icon="cross"
    48→              iconSize={16}
    49→            />
    50→          </TopBar>
    51→          <SidebarContent data-testid="sidebar-content">{children}</SidebarContent>
    52→          {footer && <SidebarFooter data-testid="sidebar-footer">{footer}</SidebarFooter>}
    53→        </SidebarContainer>
    54→      </CSSTransition>
    55→
    56→      <CSSTransition
    57→        in={isOpen}
    58→        timeout={300}
    59→        classNames="backdrop"
    60→        unmountOnExit
    61→        nodeRef={backdropRef}
    62→      >
    63→        <Backdrop data-testid="Sidebar-backdrop" onClick={onClose} />
    64→      </CSSTransition>
    65→    </>
    66→  )
    67→}
    68→

import styled from 'styled-components'

import { PageTemplate } from '../../templates/PageTemplate'
import { categories } from '../../stub/categories'

import { RestaurantsSection } from './components/RestaurantsSection'
import { CategoriesSection } from './components/CategoriesSection/CategoriesSection'
import { Banner } from './components/Banner'
import { AwardWinningSection } from './components/AwardWinningSection'

const Spacing = styled.div`
  margin-bottom: 4.5rem;
`

export const HomePage = () => (
  <PageTemplate>
    <Banner />
    <Spacing />
    <RestaurantsSection title="Our favorite picks" />
    <Spacing />
    <AwardWinningSection />
    <Spacing />
    <CategoriesSection categories={categories} />
    <Spacing />
  </PageTemplate>
)

import styled, { useTheme } from 'styled-components'

const StyledSVG = styled.svg`
  width: 100px;
  height: 100px;
  position: absolute;
  left: 50%;
  bottom: 50%;
  transform: translate(-50%, -50%);
`

export const Spinner = () => {
  const { color } = useTheme()
  return (
    <StyledSVG
      xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
      viewBox="0 0 100 100"
      preserveAspectRatio="xMidYMid"
    >
      <text transform="translate(-50 0)" fill={color.primaryText}>
        Looking for some food...
      </text>
      <animate attributeName="opacity" dur="1s" keyTimes="0;0.4;1" values="0;0;1" />
      <g>
        <circle cx="60" cy="50" r="4" fill="#22aca7">
          <animate
            attributeName="cx"
            repeatCount="indefinite"
            dur="1.0204081632653061s"
            values="95;35"
            keyTimes="0;1"
            begin="-0.6566000000000001s"
          />
          <animate
            attributeName="fill-opacity"
            repeatCount="indefinite"
            dur="1.0204081632653061s"
            values="0;1;1"
            keyTimes="0;0.2;1"
            begin="-0.6566000000000001s"
          />
        </circle>
        <circle cx="60" cy="50" r="4" fill="#22aca7">
          <animate
            attributeName="cx"
            repeatCount="indefinite"
            dur="1.0204081632653061s"
            values="95;35"
            keyTimes="0;1"
            begin="-0.3234s"
          />
          <animate
            attributeName="fill-opacity"
            repeatCount="indefinite"
            dur="1.0204081632653061s"
            values="0;1;1"
            keyTimes="0;0.2;1"
            begin="-0.3234s"
          />
        </circle>
        <circle cx="60" cy="50" r="4" fill="#22aca7">
          <animate
            attributeName="cx"
            repeatCount="indefinite"
            dur="1.0204081632653061s"
            values="95;35"
            keyTimes="0;1"
            begin="0s"
          />
          <animate
            attributeName="fill-opacity"
            repeatCount="indefinite"
            dur="1.0204081632653061s"
            values="0;1;1"
            keyTimes="0;0.2;1"
            begin="0s"
          />
        </circle>
      </g>
      <g transform="translate(-15 0)">
        <path d="M50 50L20 50A30 30 0 0 0 80 50Z" fill="#61d8de" transform="rotate(90 50 50)" />
        <path d="M50 50L20 50A30 30 0 0 0 80 50Z" fill="#61d8de">
          <animateTransform
            attributeName="transform"
            type="rotate"
            repeatCount="indefinite"
            dur="1.0204081632653061s"
            values="0 50 50;45 50 50;0 50 50"
            keyTimes="0;0.5;1"
          />
        </path>
        <path d="M50 50L20 50A30 30 0 0 1 80 50Z" fill="#61d8de">
          <animateTransform
            attributeName="transform"
            type="rotate"
            repeatCount="indefinite"
            dur="1.0204081632653061s"
            values="0 50 50;-45 50 50;0 50 50"
            keyTimes="0;0.5;1"
          />
        </path>
      </g>
    </StyledSVG>
  )
}

The `#.storybook/preview` import pattern is a subpath import that Storybook sets up. Since there's no `imports` field in package.json, Storybook must set this up automatically. Let me check if there's a storybook-specific config that sets this up.

Actually, CSF Factory with `definePreview` is a newer feature. The existing stories use the older CSF format. The instructions say to use CSF Factory, so I'll use:
- `definePreview` from `storybook/preview` in preview.tsx
- `import preview from '#.storybook/preview'` in stories


- `preview.meta()` and `meta.story()` pattern

Now I understand how this works. The `#.storybook/preview` path alias is set up by Storybook itself when using CSF factory. Let me start building everything out. I need to create the preview configuration with `definePreview` and MSW setup, update the main config to add static directories, add a portal root for modals, set up MSW handlers, and then write story files for about 10 components.

I'm planning stories for the basic UI components like Button, Badge, and Input, then the more complex ones like RestaurantCard and Review, followed by the page-level stories for HomePage, RestaurantDetailPage, and CheckoutPage that will need to handle data fetching and routing.

Now I need to set up mock data for the API endpoints that return restaurant lists and individual restaurant details, then build out all the story files.

content.text:
Two issues to fix:
1. **RestaurantDetailPage**: Nested Router - the decorator adds a MemoryRouter inside the preview's MemoryRouter
2. **CheckoutPage**: `getByLabelText('email')` not found

Let me fix both.
The label is "Email" (capital E). And for RestaurantDetailPage, I need to avoid nested Router. Let me fix both stories.

content.thinking:
Wait, the RestaurantDetailPage uses `useParams` to get the `id` from the route. Since the preview already wraps everything in a `MemoryRouter`, I can't add another `MemoryRouter`. But I need routes to be set up.

The issue is that the preview adds a `MemoryRouter`, and now the RestaurantDetailPage story decorator tries to add `Routes` inside it - but the initial path doesn't match `/restaurants/1`. The MemoryRouter in preview starts at `/` by default.

I need to either:
1. Make the preview's MemoryRouter accept initial entries


2. Or use a story-level approach that doesn't conflict

The cleanest solution is to update the preview's MemoryRouter to accept initial entries from story parameters, so each story can specify its own starting path instead of trying to add routing logic at the story level.

content.text: The issue with RestaurantDetailPage is that the preview's `MemoryRouter` starts at `/`, not `/restaurants/1`. I need to make the preview support custom initial routes. Let me update the preview decorator.

content.text:
Here's a summary of everything that was done: ## Setup ### `.storybook/preview.tsx` - `definePreview` from `@storybook/react-vite` with CSF Factory format - **Providers**: `MemoryRouter`, Redux `StoreProvider`, styled-components `ThemeProvider` (light theme), `GlobalStyle` - **MSW**: `initialize()` + `mswLoader` for network mocking - Supports `parameters.initialEntries` for route-dependent stories ### `.storybook/main.ts` - Added `staticDirs: ['../public']` for MSW worker file ### `.storybook/preview-body.html` - Portal root `
` for Modal component ### `.storybook/msw-handlers.ts` - Handlers for all 3 API patterns: list restaurants, get by ID, get by category - Deterministic mock data with 3 restaurants ### `package.json` - Added `msw-storybook-addon` dependency - Added `imports` field for `#.storybook/*` subpath imports ### `tsconfig.json` - Added `paths` mapping for `#.storybook/*` ## Stories (10 files, 30 story exports) | File | Component | Stories | |------|-----------|---------| | `Button/Button.stories.tsx` | Button | Default, Clear, WithIcon, Disabled, **CssCheck** | | `Badge/Badge.stories.tsx` | Badge | Default, LongText, MultipleCategories | | `Review/Review.stories.tsx` | Review | Excellent, VeryGood, NoReviews | | `RestaurantCard/RestaurantCard.stories.tsx` | RestaurantCard | Default, New, Closed, Loading | | `Category/Category.stories.tsx` | Category | Squared, Rounded | | `forms/Input.stories.tsx` | Input | Default, WithError, Filled | | `Header/Header.stories.tsx` | HeaderComponent | Default, LogoOnly, WithCartItems | | `OrderSummary/OrderSummary.stories.tsx` | OrderSummary | Empty, WithItems, SingleItem | | `HomePage/HomePage.stories.tsx` | HomePage | Default (with MSW data) | | `RestaurantDetailPage/RestaurantDetailPage.stories.tsx` | RestaurantDetailPage | Default, WithMenuSections (with MSW + routing) | | `CheckoutPage/CheckoutPage.stories.tsx` | CheckoutPage | Default, FilledForm | All stories use CSF Factory format, have `tags: ['ai-generated']`, and include `play` functions with meaningful assertions. The `CssCheck` story in Button validates that `font-family: Hind` is applied. **Results**: 14 test files, 39 tests pass. TypeScript clean.

use `userEvent` from │ the `play` context instead. │ Only use `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when you need to query outside │ the canvas, such as for portals. │ Example: │ ```tsx │ import type { StoryObj } from "@storybook/react-vite"; │ await userEvent.click(submitButton); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ The assertions should match the real pattern you copied: │ - for provider-backed stories, assert the provider-dependent UI │ appears correctly │ - for mocked-data stories, wait for the mocked data to appear and │ assert on it │ - for CSS-sensitive states, assert on visibility, text layout, │ class-driven states, or meaningful computed styles │ - for routing or navigation stories, assert the routed state or │ navigation outcome │ - for portal stories, query from `canvasElement.ownerDocument` when │ the UI renders outside the canvas │ Examples of useful checks: │ - a themed button has the expected label and is visibly enabled or │ disabled │ - a modal opened through a decorator or provider is visible in the │ portal root │ - mocked API data appears in the page instead of a loading spinner │ forever │ - a selected tab actually shows the selected panel │ - a toast, alert, or badge has the expected accessible text and visual │ state │ - a CSS class or computed style confirms the real state that matters │ ### Step 7: Prove CSS is loaded in exactly one story named `CssCheck` │ In exactly one story, named `CssCheck`, assert a component-specific │ computed style. `toBeVisible` passes on an unstyled component; a │ concrete style value proves the shared preview loaded the app's CSS. │ Pick a visually distinctive component, read a styling value from its │ source, and assert it with `getComputedStyle`: │ ```tsx │ export const CssCheck: Story = { │ args: { children: "Submit" }, │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ const button = canvas.getByRole("button", { name: /submit/i }); │ // PrimaryButton uses bg-blue-600 — fails if Tailwind / global CSS did │ not load. │ await expect(getComputedStyle(button).backgroundColor).toBe("rgb(37, │ 99, 235)"); │ }, │ }; │ ``` │ ### Step 8: Cover the patterns you found │ Write stories for the real patterns in the codebase, for example: │ - a low-level reusable component in real JSX usage │ - a provider-backed component │ - a browser-state-backed component │ - a fetched-data component │ - a real page component │ Use `App.tsx` to inspect the real provider tree and usage patterns, │ but do not make a story for `App` when the codebase has actual page │ components. │ Example page story: │ ```tsx │ import preview from '#.storybook/preview'; │ import { expect } from 'storybook/test'; │ import { ProductPage } from './ProductPage'; │ const meta = preview.meta({ │ component: ProductPage, │ tags: ['ai-generated'], │ }); │ export const Default = meta.story({ │ render: () => , │ play: async ({ canvas }) => { │ await expect( │ canvas.getByRole('heading', { name: /products/i }), │ ).toBeVisible(); │ }, │ }); │ ``` │ ### Step 9: Verify both rendering and types │ As you work, verify the stories with Vitest: │ ```bash │ npx vitest --project storybook │ ``` │ Also verify types so you catch missing required props, broken imports, │ and preview typing issues. Run the same TypeScript command the project │ itself uses. │ ```bash │ │ ``` │ After verification passes, review every changed file and remove │ anything that is not needed for the final solution, especially debug │ fixes, overly broad mocks, unnecessary dependencies, and eval │ artifacts. │ Keep iterating until: │ - every story you wrote passes │ - every story you wrote has a meaningful passing `play` function │ - the changed stories and preview setup pass the project's real │ TypeScript check │ - the rendered output looks sensible │ - the default global mocked environment is strong enough that stories │ do not need manual fetch overrides │ - stories no longer fail because the shared preview setup and story │ JSX are fixed │ - all passing stories have `tags: ['ai-generated']` in their meta │ - any stories that still need work have `tags: ['ai-generated', │ 'needs-work']` in their meta

1→import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
     2→import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
     3→import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
     4→import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'
     5→
     6→import { store } from './app-state'
     7→import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
     8→import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
     9→import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'
    10→
    11→export default function App() {
    12→  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
    13→  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme
    14→
    15→  return (
    16→    <Router>
    17→      <StoreProvider store={store}>
    18→        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
    19→          <GlobalStyle />
    20→          <AppRoutes />
    21→        </ThemeProvider>
    22→      </StoreProvider>
    23→    </Router>
    24→  )
    25→}
    26→

1→import { useLayoutEffect } from 'react'
     2→import { Routes, Route, useLocation } from 'react-router-dom'
     3→
     4→import { CategoryListPage } from './pages/CategoryListPage'
     5→import { CategoryDetailPage } from './pages/CategoryDetailPage'
     6→import { RestaurantDetailPage } from './pages/RestaurantDetailPage'
     7→import { HomePage } from './pages/HomePage'
     8→import { CheckoutPage } from './pages/CheckoutPage'
     9→import { SuccessPage } from './pages/SuccessPage'
    10→
    11→export const AppRoutes: React.FC<React.PropsWithChildren<unknown>> = ({ children }) => {
    12→  const location = useLocation()
    13→  // Scroll to top when a path changes
    14→  useLayoutEffect(() => {
    15→    window.scrollTo(0, 0)
    16→  }, [location.pathname])
    17→
    18→  return (
    19→    <>
    20→      <div id="modal" />
    21→      <Routes>
    22→        <Route path="/categories" element={<CategoryListPage />} />
    23→        <Route path="/categories/:id" element={<CategoryDetailPage />} />
    24→        <Route path="/restaurants/:id" element={<RestaurantDetailPage />} />
    25→        <Route path="/checkout" element={<CheckoutPage />} />
    26→        <Route path="/success" element={<SuccessPage />} />
    27→        <Route path="/" element={<HomePage />} />
    28→        {children}
    29→      </Routes>
    30→    </>
    31→  )
    32→}
    33→

     2→import viteConfig from './vite.config'

     6→import { playwright } from '@vitest/browser-playwright'
     7→
     8→const dirname =

    10→const playwrightProviderOptions =
    11→  process.env.STORYBOOK_TEST_SCREENSHOTS === 'true'
    12→    ? {
    13→        contextOptions: {
    14→          deviceScaleFactor: 2,

    21→      }

    24→// More info at: https://storybook.js.org/docs/next/writing-tests/integrations/vitest-addon
    25→export default mergeConfig(
    26→  viteConfig,
    27→  defineConfig({
    28→    test: {
    29→      coverage: {
    30→        exclude: [
    31→          ...coverageConfigDefaults.exclude,

    53→            environment: 'happy-dom',
    54→            include: ['**/*.test.ts'],
    55→          },
    56→        },
    57→        {
    58→          extends: true,
    59→          plugins: [
    60→            // The plugin will run tests for the stories defined in your Storybook config
